content.problemStatement:
Chef calls a sequence good if it does not contain any two adjacent identical elements.

Initially, Chef has a sequence a1,a2,…,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1​,a2​,…,aN​. He likes to change the sequence every now and then. You should process QQQ queries. In each query, Chef chooses an index xxx and changes the xxx-th element of the sequence to yyy. After each query, can you find the length of the longest good **subsequence** of the current sequence?

Note that the queries are not independent.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains two integers N and Q. The second line contains N integers a1 … aN. Then Q lines follow, each with two integers x and y denoting a query that sets a[x] = y.
content.outputFormat: After each query, output a single line with one integer – the length of the longest good subsequence of the current array.

solution.approach: A good subsequence cannot have two equal adjacent elements. While scanning the array from left to right we can greedily take every element unless it is equal to the previously taken one. Hence the maximum length equals the number of segments after compressing consecutive equal values (the number of runs). Let runs = 1 + count of i (2 ≤ i ≤ N) with a[i] != a[i‑1]. Only a change at position x can affect the relations (x‑1, x) and (x, x+1). For each query we: 1) subtract the old contributions of these two edges from runs, 2) update a[x] to y, 3) add the new contributions of the edges, 4) output runs. All operations are O(1) per query.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + Q) per test case – O(N) to compute the initial runs and O(1) for each query.
